region_of_origin: Canada (served in Singapore Malaysian Zi Char context)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03411433 covers oyster as a food item; no specific Canadian jumbo oyster entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers oysters as a category. No specific Canadian jumbo oyster sub-entry exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226808007 covers oyster as a food concept; no Canadian-specific or jumbo-specific code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One jumbo oyster piece as served at a Zi Char restaurant, approximately 80 g edible weight including shell liquor.

nutrient_source.title: Oysters, Pacific, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #15167
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw Pacific oysters (Crassostrea gigas) used as the primary nutrient reference. Canadian jumbo oysters are predominantly Pacific oysters farmed in British Columbia. Values represent raw edible flesh per 100 g. Cooking method (steaming or grilling) may alter sodium and water content marginally; raw values used as the canonical baseline per Veritas policy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Canadian jumbo oysters. Oysters and other shellfish are high-protein, low-carbohydrate foods with negligible glycemic impact. GI of 5 is assigned by analogy with other shellfish and lean seafood entries in the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables, which report GI values near 0–10 for protein-dominant seafood. Glycemic load per 80 g serving = (5 × 3.96 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.20, rounded to 0.2.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should moderate oyster intake as the natural sodium content of 417 mg per 100 g contributes meaningfully to daily sodium limits; steaming without added salt sauces is preferred over preparations with oyster sauce or soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Canadian jumbo oysters have a very low glycemic index (estimated GI ≈ 5) and only 4.95 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making them a suitable protein-rich seafood choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; the high zinc content (16.62 mg/100 g) may additionally support insulin metabolism.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Oysters provide 0.89 g polyunsaturated fat per 100 g including EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids, with relatively low saturated fat (0.53 g/100 g), supporting a cardioprotective dietary pattern; however, the moderate sodium content warrants attention in patients with heart failure or sodium-restricted diets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Canadian Jumbo Oysters are large Pacific oysters (Crassostrea gigas) farmed primarily in British Columbia, Canada, and imported to Singapore where they are served at Zi Char restaurants and seafood houses such as Marina Seafood House at Boat Quay. In the Malaysian Zi Char context, they are typically steamed, grilled, or served on the half-shell, often accompanied by ginger, spring onion, or light soy-based sauces. The flesh is plump, briny, and creamy in texture with a mild oceanic sweetness characteristic of cold-water Pacific oysters. Nutritionally, Canadian jumbo oysters are an exceptionally rich source of zinc (16.62 mg per 100 g, exceeding 100% of adult daily requirements), selenium (63.7 mcg per 100 g), vitamin B12 (16 mcg per 100 g), and iron (5.11 mg per 100 g). They provide 9.45 g of high-quality complete protein per 100 g with all essential amino acids. The fat content is low at 2.30 g per 100 g, with a favourable polyunsaturated-to-saturated ratio. Carbohydrate content is modest at 4.95 g per 100 g, yielding a very low glycemic index estimated at approximately 5. Clinically, oysters are suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their negligible glycemic impact and potential benefit to insulin metabolism via zinc. The omega-3 fatty acid content supports cardiovascular health. Sodium content of 417 mg per 100 g is a consideration for hypertensive individuals. In Singapore, jumbo oysters are priced at a premium (SGD 15 per piece at Marina Seafood House) and are considered a luxury seafood item within the Zi Char dining tradition.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw Pacific oysters (Crassostrea gigas), which is the species farmed in Canada and matches the food identity well. The match type is adapted rather than exact because no Canadian-specific jumbo oyster entry exists in any approved primary regional source. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Serving size of 80 g per piece is an estimate for a jumbo oyster; actual weight varies by harvest. 2. GI of 5 is an analogous estimate based on Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables for protein-dominant shellfish; no direct GI measurement for Canadian jumbo oysters was found in approved sources. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw Pacific oysters. 4. sugar_g is reported as 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 5. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 — naturally zero as oysters are an animal-derived food containing no plant fibre; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 6. Halal status is marked false as oysters are considered non-halal under Shafi'i school of Islamic jurisprudence followed in Singapore and Malaysia; status may differ under Hanafi school. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and all micronutrients are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #15167. 8. The food is listed under Malaysian cuisine in the source data reflecting the Zi Char restaurant context, though the oysters themselves originate from Canada. 9. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (5 × 3.96) / 100 = 0.198, rounded to 0.2.
